hypothesis: The multi-region network transitions between distinct attention contexts by passing through a high-dimensional transient state. Specifically, the effective dimensionality (Participation Ratio) of the joint VIS-AUD-PFC-MOs population activity during the pre-stimulus window will be significantly higher on early transition trials (trials 1-5 post-switch) than on steady-state trials (last 20 trials), supporting an Attractor Dynamics framework where the network energy landscape flattens before settling into a new low-dimensional basin.
novelty_justification: The Attractor Dynamics framework predicts high-dimensional transients during context switching, but no prior study had measured multi-region joint Participation Ratio during context block transitions in a multimodal task. This result quantifies a predicted but empirically untested phenomenon — and finds the opposite direction.

dataset_context.task: Allen Institute Dynamic Routing — visual/auditory context-switching, lick-response

opposing.title: Controlling brain dynamics: Landscape and transition path for working memory
opposing.authors: Leijun Ye, Jianfeng Feng, Chunhe Li
opposing.claim: The energy landscape and transition path approach provides a possible route to address these challenges.

opposing.title: Metastable attractors explain the variable timing of stable behavioral action sequences
opposing.authors: Stefano Recanatesi, Ulises Pereira, M. Murakami et al.
opposing.claim: This implies that behavioral variability arises from sources within the brain, but the origin and mechanics of these processes remain largely unknown.

opposing.title: Task-dependent recurrent dynamics in visual cortex
opposing.authors: Satohiro Tajima, Kowa Koida, Chihiro I. Tajima et al.
opposing.claim: Here we report a line of evidence that flexibly modulated attractor dynamics during task switching are already present in the higher visual cortex in macaque monkeys.

opposing.title: Quantifying the Landscape of Decision Making From Spiking Neural Networks
opposing.claim: The underlying energy landscape provides a global picture for the dynamics of the neural decision making system and has been described extensively in the literature, but often as illustrations.

opposing.title: Simultaneous embedding of multiple attractor manifolds in a recurrent neural network using constrained gradient optimization
opposing.claim: In some cases, it is thought that the synaptic connectivity supports multiple attractor manifolds, each mapped to a different context or task.

contextual.title: The dimensionality of neural representations for control.
contextual.authors: David Badre, Apoorva Bhandari, Haley Keglovits et al.
contextual.claim: At the heart of theories of cognitive control is a control representation that enables the same input to produce different outputs contingent on contextual factors.

contextual.title: The spectrum of covariance matrices of randomly connected recurrent neuronal networks with linear dynamics
contextual.claim: Here we study the connectivity-dynamics relation as reflected in the distribution of eigenvalues of the covariance matrix of the dynamic fluctuations of the neuronal activities, which is closely related to the network dynamics’ Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and the associated effective dimensionality.

contextual.title: Estimating the dimensionality of the manifold underlying multi-electrode neural recordings
contextual.authors: Ege Altan, S. Solla, L. Miller et al.
contextual.claim: For example, motor areas of the human brain contain tens of millions of neurons that control the activation of tens or at most hundreds of muscles.

contextual.title: A theory of multineuronal dimensionality, dynamics and measurement
contextual.authors: P. Gao, Eric M. Trautmann, Byron M. Yu et al.
contextual.claim: Di-mensionality reduction methods reveal a striking simplicity underlying such multi-neuronal data: they can be reduced to a low-dimensional space, and the resulting neural trajectories in this space yield a remarkably insightful dynamical portrait of circuit computation.

contextual.title: A scale-dependent measure of system dimensionality
contextual.authors: Stefano Recanatesi, S. Bradde, V. Balasubramanian et al.
contextual.claim: Here, we propose a scale-dependent generalization of a classic enumeration of latent variables, the Participation Ratio.

contextual.title: The geometry and dimensionality of brain-wide activity
contextual.authors: Zezhen Wang, Weihao Mai, Yuming Chai et al.
contextual.claim: By recording whole-brain calcium activity in larval zebrafish during hunting and spontaneous behaviors, we find that the shape of the neural activity space, described by the neural covariance spectrum, is scale-invariant: a smaller, randomly sampled cell assembly resembles the entire brain.

known: Neural population activity in single cortical areas is typically low-dimensional, with a few principal components capturing most variance (Gao et al. 2017; Cunningham & Yu 2014).
The Participation Ratio (PR) is a validated measure of effective dimensionality derived from PCA eigenvalue spectra (Recanatesi et al. 2020).

Attractor dynamics models predict that stable cognitive states correspond to low-dimensional attractors, with transitions between states requiring traversal of higher-dimensional landscape regions (Mazzucato 2022; Tajima et al. 2017).
Context-dependent attractor modulation during task switching has been demonstrated in macaque higher visual cortex (Tajima et al. 2017).
unknown: Whether the joint multi-region dimensionality (pooling VIS, AUD, PFC, MOs) increases or decreases during context transitions in mice performing multimodal tasks — the attractor dynamics prediction (high-D transient) has not been empirically tested at the multi-area level.
Whether the attractor dynamics framework, developed primarily in single-region recordings of primates, generalizes to multi-area recordings in mice during context-block switching.
What mechanism underlies the empirical pattern found here (lower D during transitions) if attractor dynamics do not explain it.
knowledge_map.how_this_sheds_light: The key decision node: if PR were higher during transitions (as attractor dynamics predicts), it would justify using multi-region dimensionality as a biomarker for context-switch detection and would validate the attractor framework for multi-area mouse circuits. Instead, PR is significantly LOWER during transitions (mean 13.50 vs 25.65, p=0.0065, N=5, consistent across all subjects). This is a Negative constraint: it rules out the high-dimensional transient prediction of the Attractor Dynamics framework for this multi-region circuit in mice during context-block switching. What this cannot resolve even with this result: whether single-region recordings would show attractor dynamics; whether the low-D during transitions reflects a specific functional mechanism (e.g., top-down constraint); or whether a different time window (exact block boundary, not trials 1–5 average) might reveal a brief high-D transient.
knowledge_map.caveat_impact: No triage caveats were flagged for this experiment — all 5 subjects contributed data and showed consistent direction of effect. The contribution type is Negative constraint (not Negative resolution) because N=5 from a single pilot cohort still leaves open whether the effect would replicate with different region sets or task parameters.
